title: Claim 3 proof: Tr(L_T L^dag) uses non-symmetric product without symmetrization

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap Description

Claim 3 states sum_{t in T} l(t,T) <= 2(|T|-1). The proof writes: 'By symmetry, sum_{t in T} l(t,T) = 2 Tr(L_T L^dag). Since L_T preceq L, all eigenvalues of L_T L^dag lie in [0,1], and L_T has rank at most |T|-1, so Tr(L_T L^dag) <= |T|-1.'

## The Problem

L_T L^dag is not symmetric in general (L_T and L^dag do not commute unless L_T is a polynomial in L). Referencing 'eigenvalues' of L_T L^dag and bounding Tr(L_T L^dag) via eigenvalue counts is not directly justified for a non-symmetric product.

## Correct Argument

The argument should pass through the symmetrized form:

  Tr(L_T L^dag) = Tr(L^{dag/2} L_T L^{dag/2}) = Tr(L~_T),

where L~_T = L^{dag/2} L_T L^{dag/2} >= 0 is symmetric PSD. Since L_T preceq L, conjugating by L^{dag/2} gives L~_T preceq L^{dag/2} L L^{dag/2} = Pi, so all eigenvalues of L~_T lie in [0,1]. Since rank(L~_T) <= rank(L_T) <= |T|-1, we get Tr(L~_T) <= |T|-1.

The equality Tr(L_T L^dag) = Tr(L~_T) follows from cyclicity of trace: Tr(L_T L^dag) = Tr(L^dag L_T) = Tr(L^{dag/2} L_T L^{dag/2}). This step must be stated.

## Impact

This affects the leverage averaging argument in Step 3, which uses Claim 3 applied to T = V\S.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
## Fix Applied

**Gap:** The original proof of Claim 3 referred to "all eigenvalues of $L_T L^\dagger$" and bounded $\mathrm{Tr}(L_T L^\dagger)$ via eigenvalue counts. However, $L_T L^\dagger$ is not symmetric in general (the two matrices do not commute unless $L_T$ is a polynomial in $L$), so referencing its eigenvalues is unjustified.

**Fix:** We pass through the symmetrized form via cyclicity of trace. Since $L^\dagger = L^{\dagger/2} L^{\dagger/2}$, cyclicity gives:
$$\mathrm{Tr}(L_T L^\dagger) = \mathrm{Tr}(L_T \cdot L^{\dagger/2} \cdot L^{\dagger/2}) = \mathrm{Tr}(L^{\dagger/2} \cdot L_T \cdot L^{\dagger/2}) = \mathrm{Tr}(\widetilde{L}_T),$$
where $\widetilde{L}_T := L^{\dagger/2} L_T L^{\dagger/2} \succeq 0$ is symmetric PSD. (Cyclicity: $\mathrm{Tr}(ABC) = \mathrm{Tr}(CAB)$, applied with $A=L_T$, $B=L^{\dagger/2}$, $C=L^{\dagger/2}$.)

Since $L_T \preceq L$, conjugating by the PSD matrix $L^{\dagger/2}$ preserves the semidefinite order, giving $\widetilde{L}_T \preceq L^{\dagger/2} L L^{\dagger/2} = \Pi$. Hence all eigenvalues of the symmetric matrix $\widetilde{L}_T$ lie in $[0,1]$. Since $\mathrm{rank}(\widetilde{L}_T) \leq \mathrm{rank}(L_T) \leq |T|-1$, there are at most $|T|-1$ nonzero eigenvalues, each in $[0,1]$, so $\mathrm{Tr}(\widetilde{L}_T) \leq |T|-1$.

This fully resolves the issue: the proof now works only with eigenvalues of the symmetric PSD matrix $\widetilde{L}_T$, avoiding any reference to eigenvalues of the non-symmetric product $L_T L^\dagger$.
